stimulus: Nineteenth-century philosopher John Stuart Mill is often credited with formulating the “harm principle,” the notion that the only legitimate justification for restricting an individual’s liberty is to prevent harm to others. Note “credited”: as scholar Joel Feinberg has argued, Mill’s presentation of the principle in On Liberty is in fact hedged with so many qualifications and exceptions—regarding children, debtors, and persons unable to govern themselves—that Mill cannot reasonably be said to have advanced the absolute, unqualified version of the principle that later commentators frequently attribute to him.
stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: Mill’s formulation of the harm principle has been broadly influential in political philosophy but is rejected by most modern philosophers.
The harm principle, properly applied, can resolve most disputes about when government may legitimately restrict individual liberty.
Feinberg’s analysis of Mill represents a minority view that most scholars of nineteenth-century philosophy reject.
Although the harm principle is often associated with Mill in absolute form, his actual treatment of it includes qualifications that complicate that association.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text notes that Mill is “credited” with the principle but argues, via Feinberg, that Mill’s actual presentation contains so many qualifications that he cannot be said to have advanced the absolute version often attributed to him.

Choice A is incorrect because the text doesn’t describe modern reception. Choice B is incorrect because the text doesn’t claim the principle resolves disputes. Choice C is incorrect because the text presents Feinberg’s analysis approvingly, not as a minority view to be rejected.
